title: 第 76 節
en: hymn is truly (to be considered as) the earth, for from it all whatsoever exists arises.
2. The object of its praise is Agni (fire), and the eighty verses (of the hymn) are food, for by means of food one obtains everything.

3. The hymn is truly the sky, for the birds fly along the sky, and men drive following the sky. The object of its praise is Vayu (air), and the eighty verses (of the hymn) are food, for by means of food one obtains everything.

4. The hymn is truly the heaven, for from its gift (rain) all whatsoever exists arises. The object of its praise is Aditya (the sun), and the eighty verses are food, for by means of food one obtains everything.
5. So much with reference to the gods (mythological) ; now with reference to man (physiological).
6. The hymn is truly man. He is great, he is Pra^apati. Let him think, I am the hymn.
7. The hymn is his mouth, as before in the case of the earth.
8. The object of its praise is speech, and the eighty verses (of the hymn) are food, for by means of food he obtains everything.
9. The hymn is the nostrils, as before in the case of the sky.
10. The object of its praise is breath, and the eighty verses (of the hymn) are food, for by means of food he obtains everything.
1 1 . The slight bent (at the root) of the nose is, as it were, the place of the brilliant (Aditya, the sun).

The object is now to show that the uktha or hymn used at the Mahavrata ceremony has a deeper meaning than it seems to have, and that its highest aim is Brahman; not, however, the highest Brahman, but Brahman considered as life (pra;za).
204 AITAREYA-ARAiVVAKA.
12. The hymn is the forehead, as before in the case of heaven. The object of its praise is the eye, and the eighty verses (of the hymn) are food, for by means of food he obtains everything.

13. The eighty verses (of the hymn) are alike food with reference to the gods as well as with reference to man. For all these beings breathe and live by means of food indeed. By food (given in alms, &c.) he conquers this world, by food (given in sacrifice) he conquers the other. Therefore the eighty verses (of the hymn) are alike food, with reference to the gods as well as with reference to man.
14. All this that is food, and all this that consumes food, is only the earth, for from the earth arises all whatever there is.
15. And all that goes hence (dies on earth), heaven consumes it all ; and all that goes thence (returns from heaven to a new life) the earth consumes it all.

16. That earth is thus both food and consumer. He also (the true worshipper who meditates on himself as being the uktha) is both consumer and consumed (subject and object1). No one possesses that which he does not eat, or the things which do not eat him 2.
1 As a master who lives by his servants, while his servants live by him. Comra.

2 I have translated these paragraphs, as much as possible, according to the commentator. I doubt whether, either in the original or in the interpretation of the commentator, they yield any very definite sense. They are vague speculations, vague, at least, to us, though intended by the Brahmans to give a deeper meaning to certain ceremonial observances connected with the Mahavrata. The uktha, or hymn, which is to be meditated on, as connected with the sacrifice, is part of the Mahavrata, an important ceremony, to be ii ara2vyaka, i adhyaya, 3 khaada, 2. 205 Third Khaada.

1. Next follows the origin of seed. The seed of Pra^apati are the Devas (gods). The seed of the Devas is rain. The seed of rain are herbs. The seed of herbs is food. The seed of food is seed. The seed of seed are creatures. The seed of creatures is the heart. The seed of the heart is the mind. The seed of the mind is speech (Veda). The seed of speech is action (sacrifice). The action done (in a former state) is this man, the abode of Brahman.

2. He (man) consists of food (ira), and because he consists of food (iramaya), he consists of gold (hirawmaya 1). He who knows this becomes golden in the other world, and is seen as golden (as the sun) for the benefit of all beings.

performed on the last day but one (the twenty-fourth) of the Gavamayana sacrifice. That sacrifice lasts a whole year, and its performance has been fully described in the Brahma;zas and Arawyakas. But while the ordinary performer of the Mahavrata has simply to recite the uktha or nishkevalya-jastra, consisting of eighty verses (trz'/Ja) in the Gayatri, Brz'hatf, and Ush/zih metres, the more advanced worshipper (or priest) is to know that this uktha has a deeper meaning, and is to meditate on it as being the earth, sky, heaven, also as the human body, mouth, nostrils, and forehead. The worshipper is in fact to identify himself by meditation with the uktha in all its senses, and thus to become the universal spirit or Hira7zyagarbha. By this process he becomes the consumer and consumed, the subject and object, of everything, while another sacrificer, not knowing this, remains in his limited individual sphere, or, as the text expresses it, does not possess what he cannot eat (perceive), or what cannot eat him (perceive him). The last sentence is explained differently by the commentator, but in connexion with the whole passage it seems to me to become more intelligible, if interpreted as I have proposed to interpret it.
1 Play on words. Comm.
206 AITAREYA-ARAiVYAKA.

Fourth Kha;vi>a. i. Brahman (in the shape of pra^a, breath) entered into that man by the tips of his feet, and because Brahman entered (prapadyata) into that man by the tips of his feet, therefore people call them the tips of the feet (prapada), but hoofs and claws in other animals.
2. Then Brahman crept up higher, and therefore they were (called)1 the thighs (uru).
3. Then he said : ' Grasp wide,' and that was (called) the belly (udara).
4. Then he said : ' Make room for me,' and that was (called) the chest (uras).
5. The .Sarkarakshyas meditate on the belly as Brahman, the Anwis on the heart 2. Both (these places) are Brahman indeed 3.
zh: 讚歌確實（應被視為）大地，因為一切現存之物皆由之而生。
2. 其讚頌的對象是阿格尼（火），而八十句頌詞（即該讚歌）即為食物，蓋因藉由食物，人得以獲得萬物。
3. 聖讚歌確實就是天空，因為鳥類沿著天空飛翔，人類也隨著天空而行進。其讚頌的對象是伐由（Vayu，風），而聖讚歌的八十詩節乃是食物，蓋因藉由食物方能獲得一切。
4. 這首讚歌真正就是天堂，因為從它的恩賜（雨水）產生一切存在的事物。它的讚美對象是阿迪提亞（太陽），而這八十首詩歌乃是食物，因為藉由食物可以獲得一切。
5. 以上乃就諸神（神話學角度）而論；現在轉就人類（生理學角度）而論。
6. 頌歌確實就是人。他是偉大的，他是原主（Prajāpati）。願他這樣想：我就是頌歌。
讚歌是他的口，如同前述大地的情形一樣。
8. 其讚頌之對象乃言語，而該讚美詩之八十頌句（the eighty verses）乃食物，蓋人藉由食物而獲得一切。
九、讚歌是鼻孔，如同先前在天空的情況中一樣。
10. 其讚頌的對象是氣息，而（讚歌的）八十句經文是食物，因為藉由食物他獲得一切。
11. 鼻根處輕微的彎曲，如同說，正是燦爛者（Aditya，太陽）的所在之處。
現在的目的是要說明在摩訶婆羅多（Mahavrata）祭禮中使用的烏克塔（uktha）頌歌或讚美詩具有比表面上更深層的意義，其最高的目標乃是梵（Brahman）；不過，這並非最高的梵，而是被視為生命（prāṇa）的梵。
204 愛達洛耶‧阿蘭亞卡
12. 讚歌乃其額頭，如同先前天空的情形。讚歌所讚美的對象為眼睛，八十行詩句乃食物，蓋藉由食物而得一切。
13. 這八十首詩句（的讚歌）對於提婆而言如同食物一般，對於人類而言亦然。蓋凡此眾生皆藉由食物而呼吸生存。人依食物（以施捨等方式給與）而征服此世界，依食物（以祭祀方式給與）而征服彼世。因此這八十首詩句（的讚歌）對於提婆而言如同食物一般，對於人類而言亦復如是。
14. 凡是食物的一切，以及凡是消耗食物的一切，都只不過是大地而已，因為由大地而生起所有一切存在之物。
15. 凡是從此而去的（在地上死去），天消耗了它一切；凡是從彼而來的（從天回返以獲新生），地消耗了它一切。
16. 因此大地既是食物，亦是食用者。他也（那真正的崇拜者，冥想於自身為頌歌〔uktha〕者）既是食用者，亦是被食用者（主體與客體¹）。無人擁有他所不食之物，或不食他之物²。
猶如主人靠他的僕人而生活，而他的僕人也靠他而生活。(Comra)

2 我已盡可能根據注疏家的詮釋翻譯了這些段落。我懷疑無論是在原典中或在注疏家的詮釋裡，它們是否能提供任何非常明確的意義。它們都是模糊的思辨，至少對我們而言是模糊的，儘管婆羅門人意圖藉由這些思辨來賦予與摩訶婆羅多（Mahavrata）相關的某些儀式觀行更深層的含義。烏克塔（uktha），即應與祭祀相連而冥想的讚頌詩，乃是摩訶婆羅多的一部分，這是一項重要的儀式，見於《雅若尼亞咖》（ii ara2vyaka）第一章第三分節第二段，第205頁。第三分節。
接著講述種子的起源。大梵天（Pra^apati）的種子是提婆（gods）。提婆的種子是雨水。雨水的種子是草木。草木的種子是食物。食物的種子是種子。種子的種子是眾生。眾生的種子是心。心的種子是意。意的種子是言語（Veda）。言語的種子是行為（祭祀）。過去所行之業（in a former state）就是此人，乃梵天的居所。
他（人）由食物（ira）構成，而且因為他由食物（iramaya）構成，他就由黃金（hirawmaya）構成。凡知此者在來世將變為黃金，並為一切眾生之利益而顯現為黃金（如同太陽）。

在迦婆摩耶那祭（Gavamayana sacrifice）的倒數第二天（即第二十四天）進行。該祭祀歷時整整一年，其儀式詳細記載於《梵書》（Brahmanas）與《森林書》（Arawyakas）中。然而，雖然普通的摩訶婆惹祭（Mahavrata）的執行者僅需誦唱由八十首詩句（trin/Ja）組成的烏克陀或尼什克瓦利亞讚歌（uktha or nishkevalya-jastra），這些詩句以迦耶特里韻（Gayatri）、布里哈特韻（Brz'hatf）與烏施尼克韻（Ush/zih）等格律寫成，更為進階的禮拜者（或祭司）則應知曉此烏克陀具有更深層的含義，並應靜觀默想它乃代表大地、空界、天界，同時也代表人的身體、嘴巴、鼻孔與前額。禮拜者實際上應透過靜觀默想在烏克陀的所有含義中認同自己，從而成為宇宙精神或金胎（Hira7zyagarbha）。透過此過程，他成為萬物的消費者與被消費者、主體與客體，而另一位不知此理的祭祀者則仍停留在其有限的個人領域內，或如經文所言，他不能擁有他無法食用（知覺）的事物，或無法食用（知覺）他的事物。最後這句話由註釋者有不同的解釋，但在與整個段落相聯繫的脈絡中，如果按照我所提議的方式來詮釋，似乎會變得更加明晰。
1 文字遊戲。見註。
206 愛達羅耶奧義書（AITAREYA-ARANYAKA）。
第四個開闥毗多。一、梵天（以普拉那〔prana〕即呼吸的形態）通過那個人的腳尖進入了他，而因為梵天通過那個人的腳尖進入（prapadyata）了他，因此人們把它們稱為腳尖（prapada），但在其他動物身上則為蹄和爪。
2. 然後梵天向上爬升，因此它們被（稱作）大腿（uru）。
3. 然後他說：『廣廣地握住』，那就是（稱為）腹部（udara）。
然後他說：「為我騰出位置」，那就是（所謂的）胸部（uras）。
5. 薩爾卡羅克夏斯（Sarkarakshyas）冥想腹部為梵（Brahman），安維斯冥想心臟。兩者皆為梵矣。
